title: Device Translation Tables for Printing
text: Device Translation Tables for Printing Printer device tables are a collection of sequences (control codes) associated with specific logical functions (such as bold , form feed , and init ). The sequences begin with the escape character and are referred to as escape sequences. These sequences are specific to the type of printer. For example, an HP Laserjet does not use the same sequences as a DEC LN03. Enhanced Printing Uniface provides device translation tables for enhanced printing to support features such as proportional fonts and images, which are not available using normal translation tables. A device translation table for enhanced printing includes a TYPE definition in the device section (between the BEGIN_DEVICE and END_DEVICE tags) of the device translation table. The format for this is: Environment TYPE where Environment is an identifier for a particular environment, either MSW for Microsoft Windows platforms or CHR for character mode. Uniface provides several enhanced printing device translation tables, such as P_MSWINX for printing on Microsoft Windows and P_X11 on Unix. The P_X11 table produces postscript output and can also be used on Windows. You can use these tables as guides to create your own device translation tables. Device Modes for Enhanced Printing Device modes define scaling, orientation, and number of copies. They are specified in the device translation table using the following formats: Microsoft Windows: ^NUL mode_ Number (0, ScalingFactor ) { [ Option , Copies ] } PostScript-based printers: PaperSize mode_ Number (0, ScalingFactor ) { [ Option , Copies ] } Number —mode number assigned to this device mode ScalingFactor —percentage by which you want to increase or decrease the size of the printed data Option —layout option number; one of: 0 = portrait 2 = landscape 4 = portrait draft 6 = landscape draft Copies —number of copies you want to print If the [ Option , Copies ] phrase is omitted, the defaults are 0 (portrait) and 1 (1 copy). Example: Microsoft Windows The following example specifies a device mode zero for printing with 75% scaling, landscape layout, and three copies per print command: ^NUL mode_0(0,75)[2,3] Copy Example: PostScript The following example specifies a device mode for printing A4 paper, with mode number 0, 100% scaling, portrait layout, and one copy per print command: A4 mode_0(0,100)[0,1] Copy Related Links Device Translation Tables Device translation tables handle output to screens and printers. They map Uniface logical functions onto device functions, making it possible for Uniface to support a wide variety of hardware platforms, including their output devices. Differences Between P_MSWINX and P_X11 Output For enhanced printing, Uniface provides the P_MSWINX device translation table for Windows and the P_X11 table for PostScript. However, there are differences in print output produced, especially in the way colors and images are handled. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
